title: vimeo_1191000482
displayTitle: 2026-05-08 - Māori Affairs Committee - Ngāti Ruapani mai Waikeremoana Claims Settlement Bill
topic: Ngāti Ruapani mai Waikeremoana Claims Settlement Bill
description: Friday, 8 May 2026 | Māori Affairs Committee Ngāti Ruapani mai Waikeremoana Claims Settlement Bill Hearing of evidence Submitters List: Te Whare Taupoki Ngati Ruapani ki Uta ki Tai Co-op Tahuri-o-te-Rangi Trainor Trait Craig Atkinson Ire Heyder (on behalf of James Heyder) Te Whare Taupoki
descriptionSummary: Ngāti Ruapani mai Waikeremoana Claims Settlement Bill. Te Whare Taupoki. Ngati Ruapani ki Uta ki Tai Co-op

chunks.text: Tena koutou katoa. Nga mihi ma ke tenei huihui, i tenei ata. Thank you very much everybody for your attendance today. We are now starting the formal part of the day with regards to our role as the Maori Affairs Select Committee of Parliament itself. But first of all, thank you very much for the mihi pakatone.

It's a very warm welcome to such a beautiful place that you have here itself. And one of the treasures that we have as a committee is to be able to get around the country and come to places, to your whenua, and to enjoy your hospitality, but also to see sometimes places that we've never been before.

And that's myself on this occasion. This is the first time I've had the opportunity to be here, and I can tell you I was excited to come, and I'm pleased that I have come. So thank you for the very warm welcome to your whenua here itself. So today, the process here is, is that we as a select committee, we are here to hear from those who submitted to the process of this treaty settlement that we have in front of us here.

It is usual for us to hear those that are in support of the treaty settlement, but it's not unusual for us to hear from people that actually are not in support of the treaty settlement for whatever reason they may have. So today is about us gaining a better understanding of what the submissions are that are coming towards us to take into account when we deliberate on behalf of the settlement. Clearly, there's been a long, you might argue, arduous timeframe of actually getting it to this point here.

But nevertheless, this particular stage is an important one for the Crown, because what we are talking about here is creating law, and law binds everybody towards it, including the Crown. And I take words of some of the submissions here. The settlement itself is the beginning of a rebuild of an iwi of Ngā Pu, of different places that we attend itself.

So we are very keen to hear from the submitters. Today, we are looking at hearing from five parties today. It's our convention that we always start with the negotiating group to open up the proceedings, but we also, as for fairness, is to allow them to close up at some time at the end as well.

That's what we've always done as a committee, and we feel that that is a fair way. We will also be having a hearing in Wellington also. So there are some people that couldn't make their way to here today, and for fairness, we want to make sure they have the opportunity also. So we will be conducting a hearing in Wellington in a short time as well. So the proceedings here, our convention, everything is largely convention with regards to select committees, is that we generally give groups 10 minutes to present and individuals five minutes to present.

And we like to allow a bit of time for some questions as well within those time frames. We've only got a short number today, so as the Chair, I'll be a little bit flexible, but we do still have a time to get by. And please excuse me if I have to interrupt and move on to questions or to close the submission off at that time. So aroha mai.

Now, we have a number of people that are present with us today that are from Wellington and other places as well. And of course, we heard from Matia, who spoke earlier on, and Matia is here to help us with translation. Not all of us are fluent in Te Reo. Some of us are, but not all of us are, so it's very important that we understand, particularly if you wish to use Te Reo in your presentation, and that's absolutely accepted. Absolutely.

So we have a number of other staff from Te Tari Pakatone, from the Ministry of Treaty Settlements, but also from our parliamentary services staff who help us out as a committee to make sure. There's now a bit of formality here. It is a recorded meeting, so we record the meeting. It's not Zoom, is it? It's actually just a recording today.

Obviously, usually we would actually have it publicly available. This is a public meeting, so people are allowed to come in here. Unless I feel that an individual or a grouping are causing disruption to our process and not adding constructively to it, then I have a role as Chair to manage that.

I've never had to do that, and I hope I don't have to do that today. And it's also convention. You'll see that we've got a couple of helpers here with Henare and also Jeff for security. That's normal for us. Parliamentary services who have a responsibility of looking after the welfare of us all, but including the members of Parliament, we just need to make sure that we've got ourselves being kept safe as well.

Our staff came here yesterday, and apparently they moved these tables around a number of different ways to try and get the best positioning to be able to conduct this. So thank you to the staff for the work you've done, and I'm sure it will work fine. So with that, let me make sure there's nothing else I need to say.

I think I've covered most of it itself. Thank you once again. We look very much forward to hearing what you have. And first up, as I said before, we've got Te Pari Taupoki, who have been doing the negotiations and all that, and we'll open with your submission to begin with. So aroha.

Kia ora koutou. So I'm submitting on behalf of the Te Pari Taupoki, which isA trust established to receive the Deed of Settlement on behalf of Ngāti Rūpani Mai Waikarimoana. Ngāti Rūpani Mai Waikarimoana Settlement Trust submits strong support of the Ngāti Rūpani Mai Waikarimoana Claims Settlement Bill, and urges the Justice Committee to recommend that it's passed without amendment.

This settlement has been a long time coming, going on 40 years. Our people have waited generations for the Crown to acknowledge what the Crown did to our people. The Deed of Settlement represents a hard-won negotiated agreement made in good faith, and the bill gives it legal effect.

We ask Parliament to honor that agreement. The trust was established specifically to receive the settlement and hold its benefits in trust for current and future generations of Ngāti Rūpani Mai Waikarimoana. We are ready, our governance is in place, and our pathway towards it is prepared, and our people are waiting.

The historical account in the Deed records plainly and permanently the serious Crown wrongs committed against Ngāti Rūpani Mai Waikarimoana. These include armed Crown attacks onto Kōpani and Onepoto in the 1860s. Its scorched earth tactics that destroyed our kaimei food supplies.

Coercive and unlawful land dealings that stripped Ngāti Rūpani Mai Waikarimoana of our Waikarimoana lands under pressure and duress. Broken promises of self-government under the Te Urewera Reserve arrangements. And decades of dispossession that left our people virtually landless and severed from our ancestral rohe.

The Crown's formal acknowledgments and apology in the Deed recognizes these breaches of the Treaty of Waitangi and the profound harms they caused. Loss of life, loss of land, cultural dislocation, and long-term socioeconomic damage across generations.

That is not simply a legal formality. The public record and apology are vital for our people. They validate the lived experience of our kaumatua and confirm the truth of what happened. They acknowledge the intergenerational harm, trauma that continues to affect our whānau today.

They provide a foundation for restoring honor and building a just, ongoing relationship between the Crown and Ngāti Rūpani Mai Waikarimoana. Most importantly, this history, told and recorded by us, becomes a permanent record. Our mokopuna will always know who they are, where they come from, and why this redress matters.

The cultural redress in this settlement is significant and in important respects unique. The transfer of lands into Te Urewera reflects a clear and principled shift away from the Crown's model of private ownership to a framework that recognizes Te Urewera as a legal entity in its own right, and affirms Ngāti Rūpani Mai Waikarimoana's role as kaitiaki, which has always been a truth.

Our mana and kaitiakitanga over this rohe were never extinguished. They were suppressed by Crown actions. The Te Urewera Act model corrects that. Whakapapa becomes the legal foundation for access, use, and responsibility, entirely consistent with how our tūpuna understood and exercised authority over this rohe since time immemorial.

The settlement also transfers specific sites, including Turiōkahu and Kaitawa, as Crown properties into Te Urewera. These sites provide places for housing and reconnection to our whānau. The governance arrangements embed our decision-making and tikanga alongside statutory protections, enabling conservation, visitor use, and community needs to be managed together in a way that reflects our values.

In short, the settlement gives legal expression to what has always been true, that Ngāti Rūpani Mai Waikarimoana, as a hapū of Tūhoe, holds responsibilities of kaitiakitanga within Te Urewera. It creates a durable framework for that authority to be exercised, recognized, and honored into the future.

Ngāti Rūpani Mai Waikarimoana is a hapū of Tūhoe. That whakapapa relationship is not administrative, it is who we are. It defines our identity, our obligations to this rohe, and the tikanga under which we exercise our kaitiakitanga within Te Urewera. Our relationship with Tūhoe is one of whanaungatangaAnd it's from that foundation that we act as tangata whenua of Waipara Moana.

To who have supported this settlement, that support is not incidental. It reflects a shared whakapapa understanding of the responsibilities and authority that belong to the people of this rohe. It is that living relationship, not administrative structures, that will continue to guide how we care for Waipara Moana and Te Irurewa.

As required by the Treaty settlement process, we engaged with all neighboring iwi throughout the negotiation of this settlement. Those conversations were conducted in good faith and are a matter of record. Where the iwi have raised concerns about this settlement, we acknowledge those views while respectfully disagreeing.

Our authority over this rohe is grounded in whakapapa and in the continuity of our presence and responsibilities as ahika. That is not a position we arrive at through negotiation. It is not one we will depart from. Other iwi have had the opportunity through the Treaty settlement process to achieve redress to their claims.
We do not accept that our settlement is the appropriate vehicle for relitigating their outcomes.

Financial and commercial redress. The trust acknowledges honestly that no financial settlement can fully compensate for the scale of what was taken from Ngati Ruapani Mai Waipara Moana. The losses of land and of economic capacity across generations cannot be reduced to a dollar figure.

What the financial and commercial redress does provide is a foundation. It gives us the tools to begin rebuilding the economic strength and prosperity our people had before being disenfranchised from our lands and customary rights. The trust is prepared to deploy these assets purposefully, to grow them into jobs, services, and intergenerational wealth for our people.

Partners have been identified, plans are in place, and we will report transparently on outcomes to our iwi. The settlement does not end our journey. It begins the rebuilding. We wish to speak plainly to the committee about what continued delay means for our people.

We have carried this hurt for too long. The weight of unresolved historical grievance has worn down our values, our beliefs, and our relationships with one another. Each year of inaction brings more disconnection from our rohe, more lost opportunities to invest in our whanau and rangatahi, a deeper harm to the wairua of our community.

Our kaumatua have waited their whole lives, and many have passed away now. Whole lives for this acknowledgment. Our rangatahi deserve to inherit a settlement, empowered identity, and not a legacy of unresolved grievance. The bill is the first essential step toward that restoration.

Passing it now means we can begin the real work. Healing relationships, restoring kaitiakitanga over Waipara Moana, strengthening our whanau well-being, and building a stable future. Further delay will deepen the harm. There is no benefit for the Crown or to our people in prolonging the process.

The Ngati Ruapani Mai Waipara Moana Settlement Trust respectfully requests that the Justice Committee recommend that the Ngati Ruapani Mai Waipara Moana Claims Settlement Bill be passed without amend, acknowledging its report, the significance of the historical record, and Crown apology to the people of Ngati Ruapani Mai Waipara Moana.

Prioritize the bill's passage so that the work of restoration and rebuilding can begin without further delay. I will take a couple of short questions if there are any now, but understanding we'll have another opportunity at the end of our hearing to ask further questions.

But are there any immediate questions that we have from members at this time? Kei te pai. Yeah? Ripeka. Ripeka. These questions from the floor. Are you asking a question? Yes. Unfortunately, well, the process is here for us to ask questions. We don't turn into cross-examination.

I meant is Ripeka part of one of the other submissions? She's part of our submission. Yeah, so you'll get an opportunity to talk to what you wish to then, okay? So but with regards, thank you very much, Kara, for that verbal submission itself, and we'll be seeing you again later on. So thank you. Kia ora. Kia ora. Kia ora.

So we'll move now to Ngati Ruapani Ki Uta ki Tai Co-op and Dr. Rapata Wiri. Tena koe. Kia ora koutou. So Rapata, we've got 10 minutes allocated there. So you use that how you see fit. We have had the opportunity to read the written submission, but you use the time as you wish to. Kia ora.

My name is Dr. Rapata Wiri, and I speak on behalf of Ngati Ruapani Ki Uta ki Tai Cooperative Society. I am one of seven directors. We were set up under the Companies Act to unify all Ngati Ruapani from Waikaremoana to Wairoa to Gisborne, because our tipuna Ruapani is actually from Gisborne.

The current submitters, Te Whare Taupoki, represent Ngati Ruapani mai Waikaremoana, affectionately referred to by Alistair Best in his book, "Tuhou: Children of the Mist." We represent Ngati Ruapani ki Waikaremoana who are effectively the children who got missed out.

So the difference between mai Waikaremoana and ki Waikaremoana is fundamental to this proposed bill. First, we vehemently oppose the proposed bill passed on the fact that its foundations, its framework, are seriously flawed. In particular, I draw your attention to Te Whare Taupoki submission which states that Ngati Ruapani is a hapu of Tuhou.

We have proven beyond all reasonable doubt in the Waitangi Tribunal hearing of 2004 that Ngati Ruapani are not a hapu of Tuhou. Ruapani, this lodge here, was named after Ruapani as a male ancestor from Gisborne who came here. He's six generations from the waka Horouta which landed in Gisborne.

Kia ora. Tuhou comes from another waka called Matatua. Tuhou comes from Whakatane. Kia ora. So there is no whakapapa connection- Kia ora... between Ruapani and Tuhou. Ae. So from the outset, the submission by Te Whare Taupoki is flawed in terms of its own whakapapa.

To clarify, there are two entities at the core of this debate. This settlement, proposed settlement only caters for those Ruapani who whakapapa to Tuhou, and does not cater for those Ruapani who whakapapa to Kahungunu to all the land in between here and Wairoa to Gisborne, and who have land rights in the six blocks that are being transferred to Te Urewera Board, which is a Tuhou board.

That's what's proposed under this bill. Therefore, this bill must be urgently halted. This bill is inconsistent with the Crown's treaty obligations of active protection and good faith. The whakapapa and history of the submitters, Te Whare Taupoki, who I've just discovered are the PSGE, we had no idea they're going to receive this $24 million plus the six blocks of land, is incorrect and it is Tuhou centric.

By passing this bill, you are validating false whakapapa and a fabricated history of Ngati Ruapani mai Waikaremoana, which is essentially a Tuhou biased history. Kia ora. Even the Maori translation of this report is Tuhou biased. It's written in the Tuhou dialect.

We oppose the bill vehemently as we argue that false whakapapa and false historical accounts cannot be validated by the Crown or its agencies or by its ministers. In their whakapapa and historical accounts which are documented in this settlement, the history of Ruapani, Ngati Ruapani, is confused and influenced by an ideology called Te Mana Motuhake o Tuhou which states, believes that Ngati Ruapani is a hapu of Tuhou, fought alongside Tuhou to define this beautiful Te Urewera,

Te Rohe Pōtae o Tuhou, the Urewera District Native Reserve in 1896. However, if you read their historical account, the history that they have written fails to acknowledge that Ngati Ruapani withdrew from Te Rohe Pōtae o Tuhou in 1899. We withdrew, and we formed our own Mana Motuhake as Ruapani in 1899 and again in 1917 with the Urewera Consolidation Scheme when we formed the reserves. The 12 reserves up at Lake Waikaremoana

are only for Ngati Ruapani ki Waikaremoana. Tuturu Ngati Ruapani. 317 Ngati Ruapani owners. We have a list of owners. I have argued before the Maori Land Court last year when the submitters, the guy who did your pohiri this morning, said that the meeting house up the land, the marae up here belongs to Tuhou.

Well, I objected to that based on the fact that Te Mana Motuhake o Tuhou is nothing more than ideological mumbo jumboTherefore, the whakapapa and historical account presented by Te Whare Taupoki must not be validated by the select committee.

I ask the select committee to pause. Kaiwhakahawe. We oppose the transfer of the six blocks to Te Urewera Board and argue that this bill contradicts Tikanga Māori. And I refer to case law. Case law, the Supreme Court, the case of Pakinekora and Te Kaunihera Kaumatua o Tūhoe versus Te Wī Taumataua in 2023, where the Supreme Court ruled that Tikanga cannot be its own judge.

This is a Supreme Court decision, therefore breach of Tikanga cannot be validated by this select committee. This bill is also a breach of tino rangatiratanga guaranteed in Article 2nd of the Treaty, and mana motuhake, which Mr Trader Tate and I, my uncle, argued before the Constitutional Kaupapa Inquiry in Palmerston North last year.

You can read our evidence at pages 31 and 32 of the draft report of the Constitutional Kaupapa Inquiry, where we rejected the constitutional legitimacy of unilateral Crown sovereignty, which allows laws such as this proposed bill to be passed without consultation.

This proposed bill creates enduring legal prejudice. We also oppose the bill because the Crown has relied on one group's mandate, Ngati Ruapani mai Waikaremoana, the Children of the Mist. The Crown has failed to properly resolve overlapping claims with our whanau, with our hapu, and the six blocks that have been transferred to Te Urewera Board.

This is inconsistent with tribunal guidance that overlapping interests must be properly addressed, not overridden or ignored as some sort of intertribal dispute. In that submission, Te Whare Taupoki claims that the history is plain and permanent.

However, we argue that the history is not as plain and permanent and simple as they might lead you to believe. The historical account they have provided is very much an unsettled history. The historical account is Tūhoe-centric. Tūhoe-centric.

Tūhoe bias treats Ngati Ruapani as a hapu. We are not a hapu of Tūhoe. We are an iwi in our own right. The historical account has not been agreed upon by all affected parties. You heard I read the submissions from our cousins from Ngati Kahungunu, Tatau-Tatau.

Karahora. Karahora. We have one here who's going to give evidence today. At risk of becoming legislated history, this creates a dangerous precedent because disputed historical narratives become permanently embedded in law, and I believe in the rule of law.

I gained a Master of Laws from Victoria University. I don't believe in the law of the jungle. I believe in the rule of law. We oppose the bill because Te Urewera Board and land authority issues. There are issues with the board. The bill embeds redress within Te Urewera framework, effectively reinforcing Tūhoe governance structures, limits recognition of other iwi authorities and PSTEs such as Tatau-Tatau.

It locks in a fabricated interpretation of mana whenua, and that is based on lies and the manipulation of whakapapa. Dr. Witty, could you just point out any new outcomes? We've sort of covered a little bit that you- Yeah... a bit repetitive, but if you could just point out- Okay... any new stuff you've got there.

So, just very quickly. So we've been denied equal recognition. We're structurally excluded. The prejudice is severe and irreversible, and the bill will cause permanent loss of mana whenua, loss of treaty claim rights, distortion of whakapapa, and entrenchment of incorrect historical narratives.

If I may just talk briefly about delay. Very briefly, yes. Very briefly. Te Whare Taupoki argues that delay will cause us harm. We oppose this because we believe that a rushed settlement will create a flawed settlement, and a flawed settlement will cause far greater harm than any delay. Recommendations, and I close up here.

Yeah. We respectfully request that this bill will not and cannot proceed in its current form. We request that an independent review of whakapapa assertions, historical account, mandate, and agreement in principle. We request genuine engagement with Ngati Ruapani ki Waikaremoana claimants and community, as well as Tatau-Tatau and other affected PSTEsWe respectfully urge the select committee to delay and pause the progress of the bill until these concerns have been independently examined and resolved.

A treaty settlement founded upon unresolved grievances cannot deliver true and enduring justice. We are not opposed to treaty settlements, but we urge the select committee to pause on passing this bill in order to make the foundations of this treaty settlement more solid, more robust, more transparent, and fairer for everyone concerned.

Kia ora. Ngā mihi. Thank you very much. Just before you move off this, are there any questions of Dr. Ruby? Yes. Steve? Hey, got a question. Steve. Yeah. Kia ora. Kia ora. I appreciate that. You would know from the legal process around these settlements that the effect of the Māori Affairs Select Committee's responsibility is to formalize a deed of settlement that's been already signed.

And traditionally, it's peculiar or unusual to delay this, so you're making the case for an exceptional circumstance. Obviously, there's often disagreement within iwi about whether a settlement should be made, whether it's sufficient, and we understand, we are acutely aware at least that, I feel acutely, the divisive character of settlements often. It's why I've just stood market want to, because I've circulated that divisive settlement processes.

Mm-hmm. I wonder if understanding your profoundly expressed concerns, do you think there's a version of meaningful amendments that could be made that would resolve them, or is it really the fundamental character of it which places Maere Ruapani as a part of Tuhoe when you believe that Ruapani should be as independent to Tuhoe?
The settlement should be separate from any association with the Tuhoe reo.

So I feel like that was really a clarification for understanding. I think you made it pretty clear. Yeah. Thank you. Kia ora. Okay. That's order. Kush? Kushal? Tena koe, otirā, tēnā tatou katoa kua huihui mai i tēnei rā. You mentioned the requirement for clarification of whakapapa.

Yes. And I think we can all agree that that is not the role of the Crown or this select committee. How do you envisage that happening, that process, and who might that group be? I think there needs to be meaningful engagement between Ngati Ruapani mai i Waikaremoana or te whare kaupiki and Ngati Ruapani ki Waikaremoana and tatou katoa to address those overlapping issues of whakapapa.

They haven't been meaningfully discussed. And Ira will talk more about that. That korero hasn't occurred. So you need a period of time, you're requesting a period of time for an independent process driven by respective hapu iwi. Aye. What might that timeframe be?

Six months. Kia ora. Tena koe. Any last questions of Dr.? Kia ora, Doctor. Thank you very much for your impassioned submission. We appreciate it. Thank you. And I think you've given us a lot of thought to be considered in our deliberations we're heading into.

Kia ora. Kia ora mai. Kia ora. Kia ora koutou. Okay. Can we now move to Tāhuri o te Rangi, Traynor Tate? Tena koe. Kia ora koutou. Traynor, we actually have you down for- Sorry, I'm a bit- Sorry. We actually have you down for five minutes as an individual, but like I said before, I'll be a little bit flexible.

But, we've had the opportunity to read your written submission. So you point out the key points you want us to understand. So kia ora mai. Kia ora. I don't think it's going to last more than five minutes, but however, opposition to the bill. I'm sorry, I've written everything in te reo.

Hey, ae. Hei, hei te pai. Nā tēnei pire e tono ana kia uru mai te papahātepai ki te whakahaere mō Te Urewera. He tino māharahara tēnei. He hoki whakamuri tēnei. He whakahokinga mai i te mana ki te karauna. He whakaiti i te mana o te tangata whenua.

Kāore tēnei i te hāngai ki te wairua o Te Urewera hei tangata. Kāore hoki i te hāngai ki te whakaaro o te rangatiratanga. Rangatiratanga and whakapapa. He uri nō tēnei whenua. He tino māharahara ana ahau i te whakaiti i te mana o te tangata whenua.

Ko ngā whakatau mō Te Urewera, me noho ki te hunga i whai whakapapa ana ki a ia. Kāore he tikaKia nekeia atu taua mana ki te Crown. Via representation. Nei tētahi take nui. Kāore he māngai mō te rohe o Wairoa ki runga i te poari o Te Urewera.

Kāore he reo. Ahakoa ngā hononga whakapapa, ahakoa ngā kōrero tuku iho, kāore mātou i te tēpi. Kāore e tika kia whakarere kēhia te hānganga mēnā kāore anō kia whakatikahia tēnei raru. Te Tiriti o Waitangi. E pā ana hoki tēnei ki Te Tiriti o Waitangi. Kei hea te rangatiratanga, te whakawhanaungatanga, te tiakitanga o te mana whenua?

Ki ahau nei kāore e kitea ana i tēnei pire. I roto i ō mātou hapori, kei reira tonu ngā mamae o mua. Ngā wā i tangohia ai te mana, ngā wā kāore mātou i rango. Ae, kei te kite anō mātou i aua āhuatanga i roto i tēnei pire.

Ko tāku nei i hiahia. Nō reira, e te komiti, kaua e whakaae ki tēnei pire i tēnei āhua, engari whakaritea he māngai mō te rohe o Wairoa. Waiho te mana whakahaere ki te tangata whenua. Whai i ngā mātāpono o Te Tiriti o Waitangi. Hei whakakaci i tēnei kōrero, ko Te Urewera ehara i te rawa, he tangata, he tipuna.

Ko mātou ngā uri. Kei a mātou te kawenata ki te tiaki i a ia. Kāore au e whakaae kia whakaitihia tōna mana. Kua mutu taku kōrero. Mēnā ka whakaitihia Te Urewera, ka whakaitihia hoki mātou. Kāore mātou e whakaae ki tēnā. Nō reira, tēnā koutou, tēnā koutou. Kia ora tātou katoa.

Kia ora. Kia ora. We'll just go to Kushla for a question. Tēnā koe. Kei te rongo koe? Yeah. Okay. Tēnā koe mō o kōrero i tēnei ata. He anō ra, mehemea ka uru atu tētahi nō te Wairoa ki runga i tēnei poari, arā ka noho tētahi o te Wairoa ki te tēpū, me kī, ka tautoko koe i tēnei pire?

Kare e taea te aukatia ki a rātou e honohono rawa ō rātou ki a mātou i kōnei i Whakaaru Moana, i runga rā i ngā temata o te whenua. Kare taea te aukatia atu i ō rātou whenua kei te whakatuke ki ētahi atu. Nō reira, mēnā ka whai tūru tētahi o koutou ki runga i te poari o Te Urewera, ka tautoko koe i te pire?
Me pēnei pea te kōrero ka maru ake tā mātou pai i te tika.
Okay. Any other questions from members?

China, you've given us a very good written submission as well as your verbal submission. Thank you. I think it's reiterating what your whanaunga has submitted as well. So, kia ora mai for your submission today. Kia ora. And the Māori version will be easy.

Kia ira anō au question. All right. Craig Atkinson. Welcome, Craig. Kia ora. I had a chat with Craig earlier on. He has traveled over two hours to be with us today . So kia ora mai, Craig, for making the effort to come here, and you've contributed to many treaty settlements, and I'm appreciative of that.

But you understand the process here. We've got five minutes allocated for your time, so haere atu. Kia ora. First of all, I'd just like to acknowledge from the mountains, the hills, the lands, the rivers, the streams that make the magnificent place that we sit here today. I'd like to acknowledge all the people that have gone before, that had the persecution and the tyranny from the Crown, all the people that have been left to fight for years and years and years in a process that's not fit process, a process that divides

Māori, and that. I'd like to acknowledge all the people that go after, all the young people, as we see running around, and this is what these treaty claims, I believe, is about, what is left for Māori, and that to end poverty, to end homelessness, and that there's so much we could achieve from these treaty claims and so much we fail.

And of the basis of it, the Crown divides the local iwis, hapūs. They have them arguing amongst themselves that they can't come to an agreement because it's not a full process. That's part of your game. You sit there and sort of smile and say, "Oh, yeah," but it's yourselves that do it. You rubber stamp it.

You're all under contract. You're all paid very well by the Crown and that. So there is no neutrality here. There is numerous conflicts of interest by the committee, and the process, and the figure that you come up with, 0.07%, that they get anything that's under a zero is a negative in my view. The land is never commercially feasible. The only land that you ever give back is actually what will cost the Crown money So it's just balancing its book by returning, which is a token gesture.

The apology is just not acknowledging, an apology is not worth nothing. It's got no value whatsoever. So yeah, the entire process is flawed for Maori. At the end of it, there is no natural justice. And it's all set to divide Maori and things.

We've just seen farmers have a payout of 500,000 per household handed out. How much can we say each iwi here, each individual person, how much is this Treaty claim going to put in their family? I'd say zero. None of them will ever see any of it. So for that point, it doesn't empower Maori and it does nothing more than just put that this is just another breach of what the Treaty was actually set out in the beginning.

So, ana. Now, Wairateia, start please. So kia ora, Mr. Chair. Kia ora committee members. So my name is Wairateia Moe. I am employed by the Ngāti Ruapani Mai Wakaremoana Trustees, and I have whakapapa to Ruapani through my puku h**e and my grandfather, Hi Kaiata Moe.

So I want to respond to some of the questions that have arised in the submissions. So historical account is Tūhoe centric. This historical account truthfully describes the atrocities suffered by the Ruapani people. The historical account is a shared historical narrative because the histories of Tūhoe, Ruapani, and Kahungunu are deeply intertwined through whakapapa and shared events. So a degree of overlap in the narrative is unavoidable and historically accurate.

I also want to reiterate that we rejected the Crown translator and insisted on using our own translators for the historical account, namely Newton Lambert, Maia Rurihe, and Patrick McGarvey, who certified the translation. These men are Ruapani and they are Tūhoe.

You cannot separate that. To suggest otherwise would be like trying to chop them in half and saying, "Okay, guys, I only want the Ruapani part of you to translate the historical account. The Tūhoe part can go and sit in the naughty corner." So that would be messy. It would take twice as long to translate, and we would still be negotiating that account today.

So yes, calling this as Tūhoe centric is not wrong. As our elders have said, and as Newton has told me what the elders said, that "I am Ruapani and I am Tūhoe." You cannot split the two. Okay. So this bill is fit for purpose. Throughout the negotiation settlement process, we have maintained an open door policy.

We have consistently brought our uri along on the settlement journey at the monthly Hapū hui via our social media platforms, our website, and our pānui. And we met with uri individually on several occasions at their bequest.We also consulted and engaged with our neighboring whanaunga, overlapping groups at their tari, online, in Wellington, and at our marae.

We also wrote a letter of intent to our overlapping groups, how we will come and work together on shared interests. I also want to remind Mr. Haida that we agreed that we needed to strengthen our relationships. We didn't deny that, and we agreed that those whakapapa questions that rise do not belong in the settlement process.

They belong outside of this process. This bill is fit for purpose because it does not deny anyone access. Everyone who has whakapapa to Te Urewera will continue to have access to their lands, to continue their customary rights to hunt and connect to their whenua.

The Urewera Act is inclusive and welcomes everybody to visit Te Urewera, but a PSGE does not have automatic rights here. The whenua, the six reserves that everyone has claimed on are here, they're not in Wellington. So the people presenting there, why are they not here talking to us directly on the whenua? Because that is tikanga.

This bill is fit for purpose because the majority, 82.82% of ballot votes cast on the resolution voted agreed. The voter return was 72.35% with 492 votes cast. 79.88% voted online, with 20.12% voted by post or in person at the information hui.

We now hold the highest voter rate in the mosu, and that was through hard work through the hapū here. If we pause this settlement, then the Crown will undermine our whanau who attended these information hui, which none of those outside you have listened today or Te Pari Taupoki did not attend.

This bill is fit for purpose because Ruapani Waikaremoana have waited long enough, and to pause this settlement on these flimsy AI multi-templated submissions would be wrong and would be a dark stain on the Crown. This bill is fit for purpose because it is designed with intention, and it is well considered, and it was built on the aspirations of our uri.

But more importantly, it was built so we can build a Waikaremoana that is prosperous and vibrant for our mokopuna. This bill is not fit to discuss our whakapapa. That is, as I've mentioned before, outside the settlement. We agreed on that in our hui with our neighboring whanaunga group.

This bill cannot be paused because we have work to do. Today, we need to learn to put our own personal grievances aside because our mokopuna cannot be left dealing with this 30 years down the track. And in my final remark, I want to remind our whanaunga here who have attended today, on 28th of February 2026, we signed the deed of settlement right out there.

Ruapani had many children, twins, and grandchildren, twins. And so he had a lot of descendants, a lot of uri. Most of those uri live outside Waikaremoana. They are not ahika. They have no tūrangawaewae here. The last speaker, Ina Four, he has got whakapapa. He's got whakapapa to Pukehore and to Hinekura.

So we specifically chose those three tipuna because that's what our clothes had used when they first put the claims in for the land. They were under Pukehore, Tuwai, and his sister, Hinewao. But we chose Hinekura because Hinewao's uri and Tuwai's uri intermarried about three generations down.
Those Ngati Ruapani outside of Waikaremoana, they married into Hinemanuhiri, Tamaterangi, the descendants from Pukehore, Ngati Makoro,

and Ngati InanaThe descendants of Pareoro gradually married into Tuhoe. But in the Tuhoe settlement, there's 50% of Tepungere, Ngai Tarawhaou. 50% stays with Ngati Pahauwera and 50% under Tuhoe. And they are under our claim, too.

comments myself. I often look at these treaty settlement processes and the part that we play. We, as members of Parliament, are here for a small amount of time to try and do the best we canIn what I call a process that's perfectly imperfect.

And as we've heard, I empathize with people saying that the redress is a scratch of the surface. It's a pittance of actually the wrongdoings of the past. And we see this all over the motu, all over the place back where I'm from, the Taranaki. We've seen it all there.

And I'm very mindful personally, that this process here is about providing a new beginning to rebuild from everything that has been lost, and that presents its challenges in itself, and the resource that comes from treaty settlements can be easily argued as just a pittance of what it takes to begin that journey.

But a bigger concern of mine is we often, if not always, hear from whanau nui of a settlement where there's unrest between your people. And it concerns me that the Crown is endeavoring to start again with its responsibilities and allow iwi to rebuild, but are we sometimes causing a new rauraru, a new problem within iwi? I'm very mindful of that, and I ask that question about this, and I accept the

arduous journey that's been had to get your settlement to this point. We play but a part in this process, and it's always valuable for us to be number one on the whenua. And I take your point, Waireti, about korero being here on your whenua, kanui ki kanui.

And we have to go back down to Wellington, I suppose to discuss, to query, to ask questions of people to try and help you to start again. And that is a challenge, and it's like I said, it's imperfect where we're here. But it's so important for us to hear from everybody, your whanau ma, and the different points of view, because you don't get that from reading documents.

It's so important to hear and to see and to feel the empathy, feel the emotion of what this is on both sides. Because we have a job here that is so important. A treaty settlement is an important part in your history. It will be looked back on in this process here. But it's just a point of time.

It's actually the future and what you're doing intergenerationally for everybody else in the future that we are mindful of trying to giving you a pathway, giving you some more open doors to be able to begin that new journey, and it doesn't come without its... How would you call it? Our challenges and emotions that are in, because I think we all here reflect on our own home whenua and our own home iwi and apu with what they've gone through in that. So it is quite an emotional journey that I say personally for us to actually go through this here.
